Title: Asian entrepreneurs face tougher climate amid geopolitical strains: ARCB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Asian entrepreneurs are facing a more challenging business environment due to rising geopolitical tensions and ongoing global supply chain disruptions, ARCB Asia said.
Its chief executive officer Dr Udesh Chaskar said the rapidly evolving global landscape is witnessing shifting supply chains, more selective capital flows, rising regulatory pressures and technological advancements that are outpacing many businesses' ability to adapt.
"This situation presents a dual reality for entrepreneurs, both risks and opportunities. Small businesses risk being left behind if they fail to digitalise, diversify, or build strong regional collaborations.
"However, at the same time, Asia is emerging as an increasingly important region as global companies seek alternative markets, new supply chain routes and growth hubs," he said in a statement.
Udesh said ARCB, a Dubai-based venture capital and development group, views Malaysia and Asean as being strategically positioned within the transition.
Entrepreneurs who can integrate digital capabilities, compliance readiness, regional collaboration and scalable business models stand to benefit significantly from these global shifts.
"Entrepreneurs can no longer afford to think locally alone. A regional, digital and strategic approach is now essential," he said.
In response to global economic uncertainty, Udesh said ARCB is using a resilience-based approach to help businesses stay competitive.
This includes helping companies use digital tools such as artificial intelligence (AI), automation and data analytics to improve efficiency.
Businesses are also encouraged to expand beyond a single market or supply chain, with Asean and the Gulf Cooperation Council identified as key regions for growth.
ARCB also helps entrepreneurs become more attractive to investors by improving governance, financial planning, communication, and overall business strategy.
ARCB Venture Labs head Nour Thabet said ARCB is strengthening its support for SMEs in Malaysia to help them grow digitally and expand into regional markets.
He said many SMEs have strong products but face challenges such as limited funding, low digital adoption, weak investor pitching skills and limited regional networks.
Through the Venture Labs platform, ARCB helps entrepreneurs assess their businesses and identify areas for improvement.
"Support includes digital transformation, adoption of AI, strengthening business models, access to strategic partners and engagement with investors," he said.
ARCB Group chairman Dr Mohammed Saeed Al Kindi said funding alone is not enough for SME success.
He said businesses also need strong strategy, good governance, digital systems, branding and market knowledge to ensure long-term sustainability.
He added that ARCB Venture Labs operates on five pillars to support scalable and innovation-driven ventures, such as custody, insurance, incubation, blockchain and AI.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Neighbours hear cries for help before college student found dead in fire
Article:
SUNGAI PETANI: "I woke up when I heard a commotion outside before seeing thick smoke coming from my neighbour's house," said Nurul Huda Murshidi.
The 48-year-old resident said she alerted her husband after realising the neighbour's house in Lorong Astana, Bandar Seri Astana, was on fire in the early hours of the morning, a blaze that
claimed one life
.
In the 4.30am incident, Amir Hamzah Samsul, 20, was found dead, while his father, Samsul Khairuddin, 54, was admitted to Hospital Sultan Abdul Halim (HSAH) here after inhaling smoke.
Nurul Huda said she saw a neighbour using a water jet to help put out the fire, but it was unsuccessful as sparks were already visible before the blaze intensified.
"At that time, the Fire and Rescue Department personnel had not arrived yet. We were unable to help as the fire began to spread rapidly.
"Neighbours said they heard people calling for help from inside the house, but nothing could be done as the fire spread quickly," she said.
She said three cats and a rabbit belonging to the family were successfully rescued by firefighters from the house.
She said that she was saddened by the incident as the deceased was a close friend and the same age as her child.
Amir Hamzah was a fourth-semester student at Kolej Vokasional Butterworth, Penang, pursuing a Diploma in Business Management.
The victim's mother, Hasliza Harun, 49, and the eldest child, Noor Ain Samsul, 22, as well as the youngest, Amir Haqeem, 12, were unhurt and managed to escape through the back door of the house.
Sungai Petani Fire and Rescue Station chief Siti Norafiza Mohd Noh said the department received an emergency call at 4.51am, with 80 per cent of the house already burnt.
"A 20-year-old man was found dead near the living room area in the attic section. The operation involved personnel from the Sungai Petani Fire and Rescue Station and assistance from the Aman Jaya station.
"The fire was brought under control at 5.41am and the operation ended at 9.02am before investigations were carried out by the Kedah Fire and Rescue Department's Investigation Unit," she said.
She said the victim was believed to have died from suffocation due to smoke inhalation, with only minor burns on parts of his body, while his father was taken to hospital by the Health Ministry after experiencing breathing difficulties and was placed in the red zone at HSAH.
"Five people were in the house during the incident, but four managed to escape while one died. We have yet to determine the cause of the fire as investigations are still ongoing, but it is believed to have started in the living room," she said.
Meanwhile, Kuala Muda police chief Assistant Commissioner Hanyan Ramlan said the case is currently classified as sudden death pending a post-mortem report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Food delivery rider fined RM2,500 for keeping stolen MyKad
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A food delivery rider was fined RM2,500 by the magistrate's court today for dishonestly retaining a stolen MyKad belonging to a hospital pharmacy staff member in March.
Magistrate Farah Nabihah Muhamad Dan also ordered Muhammad Naufal Razak, 36, to serve one month in jail if he fails to pay the fine.
He pleaded guilty to a charge of dishonestly retaining the stolen MyKad of a 38-year-old woman at 5pm at a pharmacy in Hospital Canselor Tuanku Mukhriz, Jalan Yaacob Latif in Cheras on March 13.
The charge, framed under Section 411 of the Penal Code, provides for imprisonment of up to five years, a fine, or both, upon conviction.
According to the facts of the case, the victim, who worked at the pharmacy, discovered that her handbag had been left open and her purse was missing at 7pm on the same day.
The accused was later arrested by police from Sentul.
Deputy public prosecutor Amritpreet Kaur Randhawa urged the court to impose a proportionate sentence as a deterrent.
In mitigation, the National Legal Aid Foundation said the accused is supporting his unemployed wife and six children, is a first-time offender who had recently started work, and earns a minimal income.
The foundation also said the accused was not remanded and had voluntarily appeared in court after being informed that he would be charged.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia aims to build advanced semiconductor packaging in two years
Article:
BATU KAWAN: Malaysia aims to develop domestic capability in advanced semiconductor packaging within two years through the establishment of the Malaysia Advanced Packaging Consortium (MAPC).
Science, Technology and Innovation Minister Datuk Chang Lih Kang said the consortium was formed through collaboration among five local companies and the government under a matching grant and "whole-of-nation" approach, combining public and industry efforts to build advanced packaging capabilities.
He said the five companies are SkyeChip Bhd, Inari Technology Sdn Bhd, FusionAP Sdn Bhd, Pentamaster Instrumentation Sdn Bhd and NSW Automation Sdn Bhd, each contributing distinct strengths to the sector.
"The government has approved a RM92 million research and development (R&D) grant over 24 months for the programme, while industry is contributing RM93 million, bringing the total to RM185 million.
"The main objective is to enable Malaysia to move up the semiconductor value chain. We already have a strong foundation in semiconductors and electrical and electronics, but we have been largely focused on outsourced semiconductor assembly and test (OSAT)," he told reporters.
He spoke after a working visit to three of the companies, joined by Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir, Deputy Investment, Trade and Industry (Miti) Minister Sim Tze Tzin and Academy of Sciences Malaysia president Tengku Datuk Mohd Azzman Shariffadeen.
Chang said the initiative is expected to strengthen local firms, attract investment, generate economic returns, create jobs and help retain local talent.
He added that the project could create a win-win outcome for both government and industry if targets are achieved within two years.
"Currently, we do not have the capability to produce advanced packaging. The public and private sectors are working together to develop this capability. If successful, the first benefit is that intellectual property (IP) will remain in Malaysia.
"Second, advanced packaging is highly niche and only a few countries can do it. If Malaysia succeeds, it will unlock significant business opportunities and accelerate economic growth," he said.
Separately, Akmal Nasrullah said semiconductor development is a key sector under the 13th Malaysia Plan (13MP), particularly in high-impact and high-value industries.
He said national development should not focus solely on physical infrastructure, but must also include investment in R&D, with both government and private sector commitment.
"The semiconductor supply chain is long, spanning design, assembly, testing and device production for end users.
"Development is not only about physical infrastructure. Through Ministry of Science, Technology and Innovation, the Economy Ministry is of the view that development investment must also include R&D," he said.
Meanwhile, Sim said Malaysia is well known for OSAT capabilities, but its packaging technology has remained largely traditional for more than 50 years.
He said Miti supports the MAPC consortium to ensure local companies develop their own IP rather than relying on multinational firms.
"The country is currently the world's sixth-largest semiconductor exporter, and the development of advanced packaging will strengthen the ecosystem and national IP as the world enters the AI supercycle.
"If successful, Malaysia will become a key hub for semiconductor packaging, testing and export," he said, adding that the initiative is part of the National Semiconductor Strategy (NSS), which focuses on advanced packaging.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL shuts Taman Miharja eatery over hygiene, drainage issues
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A food premises in Taman Miharja, Cheras has been ordered to close until all cleaning and improvement works are fully completed.
The order followed an inspection by Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL), which found that the operator had been carrying out washing and cooking activities on the sidewalk outside the premises, while also placing buckets and oil traps in parking spaces.
The activities caused the road surface to become wet and damaged, resulting in potholes.
"Two compounds were issued for offences under the Licensing of Food Establishments (FTKL) By-laws 2016 and the Street, Drainage and Building Act 1974.
"Follow-up inspections will be conducted from time to time to ensure the premises maintain cleanliness and comply with all set conditions, in the interest of public comfort and well-being," DBKL said in a Facebook post.
Earlier, a video showing parking spaces being used to wash business equipment at the food premises went viral on social media.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mercedes-Benz to recall about 144,000 US vehicles over display issues
Article:
BERLIN: Mercedes-Benz is recalling 144,049 vehicles in the US over instrument panel display issues, the US National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) said on Friday.
The recall includes certain 2024-2026 AMG GT, C-Class, E-Class, SL, CLE, and GLC vehicles.
The infotainment control unit, which is responsible for navigation and multimedia features, may reset while the vehicle is in motion and cause the instrument panel display to go blank, the regulator said.
Dealers will update the infotainment control unit software at no cost, NHTSA added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Student seriously injured in hit-and-run, police hunt for driver
Article:
PADANG BESAR: A 17-year-old male student was seriously injured after the motorcycle he was riding collided with a car in a hit-and-run incident along Jalan Semadong-Beseri here yesterday.
Padang Besar district police chief, Assistant Commissioner Mohd Shokri Abdullah, said the incident occurred at 2.30pm when the victim, a student of Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Beseri, was riding along Jalan Bukit Merah, Semadong, on his way home to Kampung Jalan Kaki Bukit.
"Upon arriving at the scene, a car is believed to have entered a junction suddenly, causing the victim to be unable to avoid it before crashing into the rear of the vehicle.
"The collision caused the victim to fall onto the road, while the car driver is believed to have fled the scene immediately after the accident. The identity of the driver and the vehicle registration number have yet to be identified," he said in a statement today.
Mohd Shokri said the victim suffered internal bleeding in the head, injuries to the chin and a fractured right leg.
He said the teenager is receiving further treatment at Hospital Tuanku Fauziah in Kangar.
"The case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987. If convicted, the offender may be fined between RM5,000 and RM10,000, jailed for up to 12 months, and have their driving licence suspended by the court," he said.
He urged the owner of the vehicle and members of the public with information on the incident to come forward to assist investigations.
Information can be channelled to investigating officer Inspector Norsyahirah Salehudin at 016-5876810 or at the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global crisis: Govt focusing on strengthening economic resilience
Article:
BATU KAWAN: The Economy Ministry is focusing on efforts to strengthen the nation's economic resilience and safeguard the supply of key input materials amidst the ongoing global uncertainty and crises.
Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said the ministry is adopting a multi-pronged approach, including addressing immediate needs while formulating long-term strategies to restructure the country's economy.
"The immediate priority is to safeguard the security of key input supplies so that economic activities can continue smoothly.
"We also refer to the National Economic Action Council which meets weekly to assess the need for government intervention to further strengthen the country's resilience in facing conflicts and global supply disruptions," he said.
He was speaking to reporters after his working visit to three companies under the Malaysia Advanced Packaging Consortium (MAPC), alongside Science, Technology and Innovation Minister Chang Lih Kang and Deputy Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Sim Tze Tzin.
Akmal Nasrullah said the ministry is also looking at ways to restructure the economy to better withstand current challenges while building new capabilities for the future.
He said the ministry's key priorities include managing the impact of ongoing global crises on the people and the broader economy, adding that the government is also focusing on long-term efforts to strengthen the country's economic structure, while creating new growth opportunities and building technological capabilities.
"Ultimately, it is about ensuring the continuity of livelihoods by keeping economic activities going, while over the longer term, continuing to find ways to strengthen and restructure the economy to create better opportunities for the country," he added.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Baru Bian seeks to strike out PKR's RM10mil bond lawsuit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Ba'kelalan Assemblyman Baru Bian has filed an application to strike out a lawsuit brought against him by PKR, which is seeking RM10 million in damages over an alleged breach of bond conditions after he left the party following the 14th general election (GE14) in 2018.
The notice was filed through Messrs YH Teh & Quek on April 6 on the grounds that the High Court of Malaya in Peninsular Malaysia does not have jurisdiction to hear the case because the cause of action arose in Sarawak.
The court has fixed May 20 for case management before High Court judge Roslan Mat Nor.
In his application, Baru Bian, who is also former Selangau MP, said he signed the bond in Kuching on April 25, 2018 and that his involvement with PKR was solely centred in Sarawak.
"The plaintiff's actions against me, even if proven true, do not arise in Peninsular Malaysia. The claim arises in Sarawak," he stated in the application.
Baru Bian is among
16 former MPs and assemblymen
being sued by PKR secretary-general Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh on behalf of the party for allegedly breaching the terms of a RM10 million bond signed with PKR.
Others named as defendants are Datuk Kamarudin Jaffar, Datuk Mansor Othman, Datuk Mohd Rashid Hasnon, Datuk Seri Saifuddin Abdullah and Datuk Seri R. Santhara Kumar.
Also named are Abdul Yunus Jamhari, Datuk Azman Nasrudin, Dr Afif Bahardin, Chong Fat Full, Daroyah Alwi, Haniza Mohamed Talha, Muhammad Hilman Idham, Muhammad Jailani Khamis, Robert Ling Kui Ee and Zulkifli Ibrahim.
In separate suits filed on Feb 19 and 27, PKR claimed that under the operative clause of the bond, each defendant had agreed to pay RM10 million to the party within seven days of triggering events such as resigning from the party, joining another political party or becoming an independent elected representative.
The plaintiff said it had nominated each defendant as a candidate in GE14, where they successfully won parliamentary and state seats, contributing to the formation of the federal government and several state governments.
However, on Feb 24, 2020, each defendant ceased to be a party member after being elected under the party banner.
"They also expressed support for political coalitions other than PKR," the party stated in its statement of claim.
PKR further stated that on March 11, 2020, the party decided to terminate the membership of each defendant with immediate effect.
Certificates dated March 16, 2020, were subsequently issued confirming the termination of their memberships, along with demands for payment of the RM10 million bond, which the defendants allegedly failed to settle.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mr DIY raises RM540mil in sukuk debut
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Mr DIY Group (M) Bhd has completed its first issuance under its RM5 billion sukuk Wakalah programme, raising a total of RM540 million.
The issuance comprises RM525 million in Islamic medium-term notes (IMTN) and RM15 million in Islamic commercial papers (ICP), the retailer said in a statement.
Mr DIY said proceeds from the first sukuk wakalah issuance will be used mainly to refinance existing borrowings, support working capital needs, fund capital expenditure and for general corporate purposes.
It added that the IMTN and ICP have been assigned initial credit ratings of AA1 with a stable outlook for the long term and P1 for the short term by RAM Rating Services Bhd respectively.
"Both indicate the second highest long term credit rating and the highest short-term credit rating assigned by RAM Ratings, respectively, underscoring the company's very strong capacity to meet its financial obligations," it said.
It said the IMTN issuance received strong investor demand, with the order book reaching a peak of 6.5 times at the initial price guidance.
Mr DIY chief executive officer Adrian Ong said the issuance marks a significant milestone for the company as it successfully accesses the Malaysian Islamic capital markets for the first time.
He added that the strategic move supports its transition towards a Shariah-compliant funding structure, strengthens its capital base, and provides greater financial flexibility to support its next phase of growth.
"We are encouraged by the exceptionally strong reception to our debut sukuk issuance as evidenced by the lower yield achieved compared to our existing borrowings.
"The oversubscription and competitive pricing achieved reflect investor confidence in Mr DIY's fundamentals, disciplined growth strategy, and ability to deliver sustainable long-term value, even amid ongoing market volatility and geopolitical uncertainty," Ong said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Couple jailed one week for stealing beer, powdered milk worth RM252
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A married couple was jailed for one week by the magistrate's court today for stealing a carton of beer and a tin of powdered milk worth RM252.30 from a convenience store last month.
The court heard that the wife, M. Vaneesri, 43, a grandmother of two, admitted committing the offence with her husband, T. Sugumaran, 38, a foreman.
Vaneesri admitted that she later sold the carton of beer valued at RM153.80 and the powdered milk worth RM98.50 to her former husband, who lives in Rawang, for RM65.
The theft took place at 8.22pm at a convenience store along Jalan Benteng Utara in Jinjang on April 26.
The couple pleaded guilty before magistrate Farah Nabihah Muhamad Dan.
The court also ordered them to pay a fine of RM300 each or serve an additional five days in jail in default of payment.
The charge, framed under Section 380 of the Penal Code read together with Section 34, carries a maximum jail sentence of 10 years and a fine upon conviction.
According to the facts of the case, the convenience store manager discovered the missing items during a stock check at 9am on April 27.
He informed the area manager, who reviewed closed-circuit television footage and found that the couple had taken the items without making payment at the counter.
Acting on intelligence, a team from the Sentul district police headquarters intelligence and operations division later arrested the couple at a flat.
During questioning, both admitted to committing the offence.
Deputy public prosecutor Amritpreet Kaur Randhawa urged the court to impose an appropriate sentence as a deterrent.
In mitigation, the National Legal Aid Foundation asked the court to impose a minimal fine, saying the couple were remorseful over their actions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heavy rain, thunderstorms expected across several states
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Heavy rain and thunderstorms are expected in several parts of the country until 6pm, according to the Malaysian Meteorological Department.
The department said the warning covers the whole of Perlis, Perak, Selangor, Negri Sembilan, Melaka, Johor and the Federal Territories of Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya and Labuan.
In Kedah, the affected districts are Kubang Pasu, Pokok Sena, Padang Terap, Pendang, Kuala Muda, Sik and Baling.
In Pahang, the warning covers Cameron Highlands, Lipis, Raub, Jerantut, Bentong, Temerloh, Maran, Bera and Rompin.
In Sarawak, thunderstorms and heavy rain are forecast in Sibu, Mukah, Kapit, Bintulu, Miri and Limbang.
In Sabah, the affected areas are the interior regions, as well as Papar, Putatan, Penampang, Kota Kinabalu, Tuaran, Ranau, Tawau and Sandakan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Queen enchanted by Uzbekistan's Islamic heritage, hospitality
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia has expressed admiration for Uzbekistan's architectural beauty, Islamic heritage and the warm hospitality of its people during her recent visit to the Central Asian nation.
In a post titled "Rahmat, Uzbekistan!" dated May 7 and shared on the Facebook page of Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar today, Raja Zarith Sofiah described the visit as a meaningful experience filled with fond memories.
"For a long time, many people, including myself, only thought of travelling to Western countries. We did not know enough about the countries of Central Asia.
"I returned to Malaysia with beautiful memories of Uzbekistan. Everyone we met showed us extraordinary warmth and hospitality. Compared with some places I have visited, the cities were exceptionally clean," Her Majesty said.
The Queen also praised the cleanliness and beauty of cities such as Tashkent and Samarkand, noting their flowers and public parks.
Raja Zarith Sofiah said she was captivated by the local craftsmanship displayed at souvenir shops in Tashkent, including intricate wood carvings, detailed paintings and colourful embroidered textiles reflecting the artistic heritage of the Uzbek people.
"There were also scarves, jackets and long robes embroidered in vibrant colours. Visiting the Centre for Islamic Civilisation was an experience I will always remember and cherish.
"I wish I had more time to see all the exhibits. The centre truly reflects the vast knowledge of scholars from the Islamic Golden Age," Her Majesty said.
The Queen also expressed admiration for the architecture of the three main buildings at Registan Square in Samarkand, saying both the exterior and interior of the mosques and madrasahs left a lasting impression on her.
"I enjoy sketching occasionally and felt humbled by the artistry and craftsmanship of those who built the structures and surrounding areas.
"The Imam Bukhari Complex once again showcased the artistic talents, heritage and traditions of the Uzbek people. I especially admired the carved wooden pillars.
"I will always treasure the moment of being at the maqam of one of Islam's greatest scholars," according to the post.
Her Majesty said the interactive museum at the complex provided deeper insight into the life and contributions of prominent Islamic scholar Imam al-Bukhari, whose compilation of hadith continues to guide Muslims worldwide.
Raja Zarith Sofiah also expressed hope of returning to Uzbekistan with her family in the future and conveyed appreciation to the people of the country for their hospitality and sharing of knowledge throughout the visit.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Teachers earn admiration for helping pupils during heavy rain [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A group of teachers has received praise online after they were seen braving heavy rain under a canvas shelter to help pupils get home safely after school.
The touching moment was shared by Threads user @nrishmyth with the caption: "Soaked in the rain for the children."
The clip has garnered more than 5,100 likes and 127 comments from netizens touched by the teachers' dedication.
View on Threads
In the
video
, several teachers were seen escorting pupils to waiting cars and motorcycles, ensuring the children remained safe and dry despite the downpour.
Some netizens also noticed the teachers shielding the pupils from the rain while getting drenched themselves.
Many users described the moment as wholesome and nostalgic, with some saying it reflected the sacrifices teachers quietly make every day for their pupils.
User @nurafaf thanked the teachers for their effort and care.
"Very cute. Thank you, teachers, for taking care of the pupils like your own," the user commented.
Another user, @zaeyka, described the experience as a "core memory".
The post's author replied: "One day they can tell their own children about it."
The clip has since resonated with many Malaysians, some of whom shared memories of teachers going the extra mile for pupils during their school days.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Political Funding Bill still under study, requires agreement from all parties - Azalina
Article:
MELAKA: The proposed drafting of the Political Funding Bill is still under study and requires the consensus of all political parties before it can proceed further.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said such an agreement is crucial as political parties would be the primary stakeholders and users of the legislation.
She said studies on political funding mechanisms are ongoing, but several policy matters have yet to reach consensus, including public funding for political parties, contribution limits and the authority responsible for regulating political funds.
"For Umno, political funding has always been a topic of discussion, but so far it has not reached the level of the Legal Affairs Division. Some political parties also raised the issue during the 15th general election.
"So the final decision really depends on the agreement of all political parties because they will ultimately be the users of the law," she told reporters.
Azalina said this during a press conference after attending the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between the Multimedia University (MMU) and the Legal Affairs Division of the Prime Minister's Department, as well as the launch of the MMU Legal Aid Clinic (MULA Clinic) today.
When asked whether the bill could be tabled before the next general election, Azalina said she could not provide any certainty.
"Before the general election? I honestly do not know. It is difficult for me to comment because I do not even know myself," she said.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PJ turns to long-term infrastructure upgrades to tackle flash floods
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Petaling Jaya is planning long-term infrastructure upgrades to strengthen flood mitigation measures as the city continues to grapple with flash floods.
The Petaling Jaya City Council (MBPJ) said it and the Irrigation and Drainage Department have outlined several structural interventions along Sungai Penchala and Sungai Klang.
The measures include constructing floodwalls at critical sections of Sungai Penchala, installing a River Overflow Control System at key outlets, and placing flap gates at smaller discharge points into Sungai Klang.
In a statement, MBPJ also said stricter drainage controls would prohibit direct stormwater discharge into rivers.
Yesterday's flash floods inundated parts of Petaling Jaya, including Sections 51, 17 and 19 along Sungai Penchala, as well as Taman Medan, Sri Manja and Buana Perdana near Sungai Klang.
MBPJ said its Rapid Response Squad was deployed to close affected roads, while KDEB Waste Management carried out post-flood cleaning operations.
A real-time WhatsApp channel was also activated to provide river water-level updates to local communities, including traders at Jalan 223.
"These measures aim to reduce the impact of recurring flash floods and improve response during heavy rainfall," MBPJ said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Fallen tree damages car, blocks traffic on Jalan Gombak
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A car was damaged after a tree fell on it on Jalan Gombak near Batu 10 following heavy rain in the area.
In a post on Threads by
@asalgombak
, the fallen tree was reported to have blocked traffic in both directions.
"A fallen tree near Batu 10, Jalan Gombak, is blocking traffic in both directions. With the current heavy rain, please plan your journey accordingly," the post read.
Meanwhile, Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director of operations Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the department received a call at 1.33pm.
"Fire and Rescue Department personnel from the Bandar Baru Selayang station were dispatched to the scene.
"The driver, a 27-year-old man, had already gotten out of the car before the team arrived and no injuries were reported," he said.
Earlier today, the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) issued a thunderstorm warning for seven states and the Federal Territories of Putrajaya and Labuan until 1pm.
Heavy rain and strong winds were forecast to affect Perak, Selangor, Negri Sembilan, Melaka, Johor, Sarawak and Sabah.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pahang must look beyond Genting, Camerons to diversify tourism, says Tengku Hassanal
Article:
KUANTAN: Pahang must diversify its tourism appeal beyond Genting Highlands and Cameron Highlands by revitalising other locations such as Cherating beach.
Tengku Mahkota Pahang Tengku Hassanal Ibrahim Alam Shah Al-Sultan Abdullah said the state has many attractive destinations, including Pulau Tioman and Cherating, that should be further promoted to holidaymakers.
"Do not depend only on Genting Highlands and Cameron Highlands. Look at Cherating and identify what needs to be done.
"Cherating was once a world-renowned destination, but why does it now seem to have been forgotten?" he asked in his speech when opening the 15th State Legislative Assembly sitting at Wisma Sri Pahang today.
Tengku Hassanal urged the state Tourism Committee chairman, Tourism Pahang and all relevant agencies to step up efforts and be more proactive in attracting visitors.
"Efforts to revitalise the tourism sector in conjunction with Visit Malaysia Year 2026 require more strategic, detailed and forward-looking planning.
"Tourism infrastructure and facilities at destinations must be further improved to attract international tourists. There are ongoing efforts, but they are still not enough," he said.
Cherating Beach, known for its
white sandy beaches and clear waters, was once a globally renowned tourism destination
.
Meanwhile, Tengku Hassanal called for investment processes to be streamlined by reducing bureaucracy, speeding up approvals and ensuring infrastructure readiness.
"The state cannot rely solely on conventional administrative channels. A fast-lane and fast-track mechanism should be introduced when a genuine and competitive company wants to invest.
"Under the normal process, land approvals may take between 18 and 24 months, but for priority investors, it can be completed within three to six months. Time is critical for investors," he said.
He expressed concern that high-quality investors could be lost to other states if approval processes remain too slow, and urged the state government to change its current practices.
"There is no need to wait three to four months just to hold meetings and approve land applications. It takes months even to complete technical reviews.
"I decree that the state government, especially the state Investment Committee chairman, review the current procedures," he said.
The Pahang state assembly sitting will begin on May 12.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah taps new technology to boost turtle conservation
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Authorities and conservation groups are now using cutting-edge technology to collect more data to strengthen turtle conservation efforts.
Sabah Wildlife Department, WWF-Malaysia and Mataking Reef Resort, which signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) for the project, will focus on data collection on the critically endangered hawksbill turtle (Eretmochelys imbricata).
The two-year project builds on long-standing conservation partnerships on Mataking Island, where the Sabah Wildlife Department, together with Mataking Reef Resort and WWF-Malaysia, recorded over 460 hawksbill turtle nests between 2009 and 2025, including 119 nests in 2025 alone.
Those technologies include ShellBank, a traceability toolkit and global database of marine turtle DNA that enables conservationists and enforcement agencies to identify populations and trace sea turtles, dead or alive, back to their population of origin.
"Complementing this, satellite tracking will monitor sea turtle movements along their migratory routes, helping to identify critical habitats and generate data for more targeted conservation action.
"By combining genetic analysis with real-time tracking, the project equips the government and conservation partners with a powerful tool to safeguard these ancient mariners across Sabah and the wider region," said a joint statement.
WWF-Malaysia Partnerships senior director Tay Mooi Geok said they had very little information about where hawksbill turtles go after they lay eggs on shores in Semporna.
"Soon, we will gain these insights, helping us better conserve them."
Mataking Reef Resort managing director Sophia Sie said Mataking Island is one of Sabah's most important hawksbill turtle nesting sites.
"As a business operating here, we are committed to safeguarding these turtles and making conservation a core part of our work, supported by our in-house team and honorary wildlife wardens."
This project, in collaboration with Universiti Malaysia Sabah and the Marine Research Foundation, is catalysed by early support from Albizia & Friends.
These efforts will also contribute to the Blue Corridors for Turtles initiative (BC4T), which seeks to map turtle movements by connecting nesting beaches, migratory routes and foraging grounds.
"By integrating genetic and movement data, BC4T aims to build a clearer, science-based understanding of distinct turtle populations across the region to inform Important Marine Turtle Areas (IMTAs)."
The MoU will also further strengthen collaborative management, integrate scientific research into practical conservation actions, and reinforce enforcement efforts to protect sea turtles from illegal trade, bycatch and poaching.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: No lifts, no choice: Family treks 36 floors in Klang Valley condo
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A family's exhausting climb up 36 floors after all seven lifts at their condominium in the Klang Valley were reportedly shut down has sparked concern and criticism online.
A
TikTok video
shared by a resident showed her, her husband and their two children making their way up the staircase after returning home, as all lifts in the building were out of service.
According to the video caption, the lifts were shut down after rainwater entered the lift shaft, forcing residents to use the stairs.
She said they had no choice but to climb to their unit on the 36th floor, with their children visibly tired from the journey.
She said that when they reached level 33A, a neighbour offered them drinking water after noticing how exhausted they were.
In the comments section, many sympathised with the children, who were seen carrying heavy school bags and struggling with the long climb.
"My goodness, I feel so sorry for the children. They even had to carry heavy school bags," wrote user @Zulhelmi Zaini.
Another user, @Yaang, commented: "I feel so sorry for the little one, crying and sweating from exhaustion."
Some also questioned the building management over the incident.
"What is the management doing like this? Residents pay such expensive maintenance fees," wrote @Irra Wonder.
Replying to another user who asked how long it took them to reach their unit, the resident said: "One hour and 15 minutes, haha."
The video has since drawn widespread attention, with many netizens debating building maintenance standards and the challenges faced by residents living in high-rise developments.
There has been no official statement from the condominium management at the time of writing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bank Negara's international reserves at US$129.7bil as at April 30
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bank Negara Malaysia's international reserves stood at US$129.7 billion as at April 30, 2026.
In a statement today, the central bank said the reserves position is sufficient to finance 4.7 months of imports of goods and services, and is 0.9 times the total short-term external debt.
Bank Negara said the gross international reserves comprise foreign currency reserves (US$113.8 billion), International Monetary Fund (IMF) reserves position (US$1.3 billion), special drawing rights (US$5.9 billion), gold (US$6.4 billion) and other reserve assets (US$2.3 billion).
Meanwhile, the central bank said its total assets amounted to RM615.55 billion, comprising mainly gold, foreign financial assets and other reserves, including special drawing rights (RM523.69 billion), Malaysian government papers (RM13.10 billion), loans and advances (RM28.92 billion), deposits with financial institutions (RM340.61 million), property and equipment (RM4.55 billion) and other assets (RM44.95 billion).
On the liabilities side, currency in circulation stood at RM184.20 billion, followed by deposits by financial institutions (RM124.98 billion), the federal government (RM4.10 billion) and others (RM70.51 billion).
The central bank's capital stood at RM100 million, with reserves amounting to RM194.26 billion, alongside Bank Negara papers (RM7.58 billion), allocation of special drawing rights (RM26.38 billion) and other liabilities (RM3.43 billion).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Toyota halves quarterly profit as Iran crisis hits
Article:
TOKYO: Toyota halved its quarterly profit in an earnings report on Friday and forecast a decline of 20 per cent in the year that just started, as rising costs and supply snarls from the Iran war outweighed surging demand for hybrid vehicles.
The results from the world's top-selling automaker highlight the lopsided impact of the Middle East crisis, with higher energy prices driving more customers to fuel-efficient cars but not in enough numbers to offset underlying cost pressures.
Toyota reported an operating profit of 569.4 billion yen (US$3.6 billion) for the three months to March 31, compared with 1.1 trillion yen a year earlier. For the year that just started, it expects an operating profit of three trillion yen.
That outlook was well below the 4.59 trillion yen median forecast in an LSEG poll of 23 analysts. Toyota shares declined after the report and ended down around 2.2 per cent at their lowest close since mid-October.
In total, the impact of the Middle East crisis will be around 670 billion yen in the current financial year, Toyota said.
The latest surge in energy prices heaps further pain on an industry already grappling with US tariffs and the rise of Chinese automakers. Volkswagen CEO Oliver Blume said this week tariffs represent a burden of 5 billion euros (US$5.9 billion) a year on the German group's operating profit.
Toyota said last week its sales in the Middle East fell sharply in March after shipments to the region were disrupted.
The outlook is the first issued by Toyota under new CEO Kenta Kon, who assumed his role last month and faces the challenge of steering the automaker through the impact of US President Donald Trump's tariffs, which cut operating profit in the year just ended by 1.4 trillion yen.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's biodiesel moment
Article:
FOR years, Malaysia's biodiesel policy has behaved a little like a plantation road after rain - passable, promising but never entirely smooth.
At times, the journey advanced with confidence.
At other times, it slowed behind the familiar potholes of infrastructure cost, subsidy anxiety, depot readiness, engine concerns and that old argument between the dinner plate and the diesel tank.
Now the engine has started again.
With B15 biodiesel production scheduled to begin from June 1 through 19 licensed plants, Malaysia is being invited to revisit a question that has never quite gone away: should palm biodiesel remain a cautious blending programme, or become a more deliberate pillar of national energy security?
The move is expected to begin with B15, progress to B20, and possibly reach B50 within two to three years.
The question matters because the world outside is not exactly behaving politely.
Crude oil prices remain exposed to conflict at Hormuz, shipping anxieties and geopolitical surprises.
Diesel imports can be expensive. Fuel subsidies are heavy. And when energy markets sneeze, national budgets tend to catch more than a cold.
Against that backdrop, Malaysia has something many countries would envy: a home-grown oil crop, existing biodiesel plants, spare capacity, and a value chain that supports both rural incomes and industrial resilience.
Yet having the asset is one thing. Using it wisely is another.
The Numbers Behind B15
For clarity, the figures matter. Malaysia's total biodiesel production capacity is about 2.4 million tonnes a year.
In 2025, actual production stood at around 1.3 million tonnes. Of this, the existing B10 blending programme absorbed about one million tonnes.
Moving from B10 to B15 would require another 400,000 tonnes, bringing total B15 blending demand to about 1.4 million tonnes.
In other words, Malaysia is not being asked to invent a new industry overnight.
The capacity exists. The plants exist. The palm oil exists.
The harder question is whether production, logistics, blending infrastructure, pricing and policy coordination can move in step.
As always, the palm oil train can move - provided the tracks, signals and station masters are properly aligned.
The cost comparison also explains why biodiesel has returned to the policy table.
CNA indicated industry estimates that diesel was close to RM6.20 per litre, while biodiesel was about RM4.50 per litre.
That spread makes biodiesel increasingly attractive when conventional diesel prices are vulnerable to global disruptions.
But arithmetic on paper must still travel through pipes, depots, engines, pumps and public confidence.
Policy may be announced at the rostrum, but implementation is tested at the terminal.
The Depot Is Not a Detail
Malaysia currently mandates B10 for motorists, meaning 10 per cent biodiesel and 90 per cent petroleum diesel.
Scaling up to B15, B20 or B30 is therefore not only a matter of asking producers to produce more.
It requires blending readiness, terminal upgrades, storage systems, quality control, logistics and confidence from vehicle users.
Here lies one of the less glamorous but very real constraints.
Malaysia has biodiesel blending depots, most designed around B10 capacity.
Upgrading this infrastructure could take one to two years, while moving towards B30 may require more than RM600 million in terminal and infrastructure spending.
This is why biodiesel cannot be sold merely as a slogan.
A mandate must eventually pass through steel, pumps, tanks, fittings and invoices. In policy, enthusiasm is free. Infrastructure is not.
Yet infrastructure should not become an excuse for permanent hesitation.
If Malaysia wishes to treat biodiesel seriously, blending infrastructure must also be treated as strategic energy infrastructure.
Otherwise, we will keep rediscovering the same problem every time crude oil turns rude.
A Crop That Can Do More
Malaysia produces roughly 20 million tonnes of palm oil annually, but only about 1.3 million tonnes is used for biodiesel.
That suggests room to think more boldly about how palm oil fits into the country's energy and industrial strategy.
For too long, palm oil has often been asked to defend itself only as food. Yet it is more than that.
It is a rural income generator, export earner, industrial feedstock, oleochemical base, biomass source and, increasingly, a bioenergy instrument.
To recognise palm biodiesel as strategic is not to abandon food security.
It is to understand that a national crop of this scale should not be trapped inside a narrow conversation.
The palm oil value chain must keep asking how every tonne, residue and side stream can create greater value.
That wider bioenergy story is already emerging.
Palm oil mill effluent, used cooking oil, palm fatty acid distillate, palm acid oil and other residues are becoming part of the broader biofuel conversation.
Biodiesel may be today's policy focus, but tomorrow's opportunity may also include sustainable aviation fuel, hydrogenated vegetable oil, bio-naphtha and other renewable pathways.
In that sense, B15 should not be viewed as the final destination. It may be better understood as a necessary gear change.
Indonesia's Bolder Script
Across the straits, Indonesia has moved more aggressively.
Its biodiesel programme is not merely green policy.
It is import-substitution policy, farmer policy, industrial policy and energy-security policy wearing the same batik shirt.
Malaysia has often been more cautious, partly because its fuel import dependency and fiscal structure differ from Indonesia's.
CNA reported that Indonesia imported 35.2 per cent of its crude oil in 2023, compared with Malaysia's 15.6 per cent, which partly explains why Indonesia has pushed harder on biodiesel.
Malaysia does not need to copy Indonesia wholesale.
Different countries have different economics, policy tools and political pressures. But Malaysia should still learn from the seriousness with which Indonesia treats biodiesel as a strategic instrument.
The question is not whether Malaysia should become Indonesia.
The question is whether Malaysia should become more Malaysian in using what Malaysia already has.
The Table Still Has a Say
Still, biodiesel is never only about the tank. It also touches the table.
The food-versus-fuel debate often sounds moral, but it is mostly economics wearing a moral tie.
Higher biodiesel demand can support CPO prices and growers' incomes.
But it can also squeeze refiners, food manufacturers, consumers and the treasury if edible oil prices rise too sharply.
That is why biodiesel policy must be honest. It can reduce fossil-fuel reliance, strengthen domestic demand, support rural income and lower emissions.
But pushed too far, too fast, it may intensify food-price pressures.
The wiser question is not whether biodiesel is good or bad. It is how far, how fast, with whose money, and under what safeguards.
Biology Still Votes
There is another inconvenient truth: demand can be mandated, but supply must be grown.
Malaysia's palm oil production has struggled to move sustainably beyond the 20 million tonne.
Ageing palms, slow replanting, labour constraints, climate stress and rising input costs all limit how far ambition can outrun agronomy.
A blending mandate can create demand, but it cannot make old palms young, or replace replanting, mechanisation, fertiliser discipline, field supervision and smallholder renewal.
In short, biodiesel can support palm oil prices. But if Malaysia wants a stronger biodiesel programme, it must also strengthen the upstream sector.
Between Promise and Prudence
Where does that leave Malaysia? Between promise and prudence.
Biodiesel is not a magic potion. It cannot abolish diesel, end fuel-price pressures or turn old palms young again.
But neither is it a decorative green label to be dusted off only during crises. It is a strategic instrument - useful, imperfect, and too important for slogans.
Used wisely, it can reduce import dependence, support growers, strengthen downstream industry, improve energy resilience and give palm oil a stronger role in a lower-carbon world.
Used carelessly, it can strain consumers, distort markets and invite subsidy headaches.
The B15 move should therefore be welcomed, but not romanticised.
The industry must be ready, depots upgraded, standards clear, the public informed, and the fiscal burden honest.
Malaysia has a golden crop. It should use it strategically, but not mistake strategy for slogan.
In the end, biodiesel is best seen not as a crisis remedy, but as a long-term national instrument - part energy security, part rural economy, part climate response and part palm oil value creation.
The tank, the table and the treasury will continue to argue. Perhaps they should.
A debate this consequential deserves clear thinking, steady execution and enough humility to remember that in palm oil, as in policy, the fruit bunch may be heavy - but the consequences are heavier.
*Joseph Tek has over 30 years of experience in the plantation industry, with a strong background in oil palm R&D, executive leadership and industry advocacy through MEOA and MPOA. The views expressed in this article are his own.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Road closures, diversions in Bukit Bintang for Quranic Madani event tonight
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Several roads around Jalan Bukit Bintang will be closed and diverted in stages tonight for the Quranic Madani programme, "Night Reset: Bersatu Dalam Saf @ Bukit Bintang."
The event, from 8.30pm to 11pm in front of Pavilion Kuala Lumpur, is expected to draw large crowds from around the capital and nearby areas.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said the closures and diversions would be carried out in stages to ensure smooth traffic flow and public safety.
"The affected route is Jalan Bukit Bintang and the closures will remain in place until 5am on May 9," he said.
He said the roads would be reopened in stages after the programme ends, depending on traffic conditions and the situation at the location.
Motorists have been advised to avoid the affected routes and to plan their journey using alternative roads.
"Road users are also encouraged to use public transportation such as the LRT, MRT, Monorail or e-hailing services to help reduce congestion in the city centre.
"Please follow instructions from traffic police personnel on duty at the location and practise courteous and patient driving throughout the road closure period," he said.
For details, contact the Tun H.S. Lee traffic police station at 03-2071 9999 or the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTS Link: Real-time monitoring for all travellers; Malaysia, S'pore agree on legal jurisdiction
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Every movement through the Johor Baru–Singapore Rapid Transit System (RTS) Link will be recorded, analysed, and monitored in real time as part of a high-tech national border security framework.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said the system, set to be the nation's busiest land gateway, will leverage advanced technology to balance seamless travel with stringent security and national sovereignty.
He said that all data collected through the system will be safeguarded under strict controls to prevent any breach or misuse.
"In a project of this scale, we are not just ensuring fast passenger movement. We are using technology to record, analyse, and monitor every movement in real time, leaving no space for smuggling, illegal entry, or other threats," he said.
Saifuddin clarified that the monitoring would cover all travellers, including foreigners and Singaporeans.
"The authority lies with the ministry. Everyone who uses the facility will be recorded and analysed," he said after inspecting the Bukit Chagar ICQS Complex today.
He said the
RTS Link
, linking Bukit Chagar here and Woodlands North in Singapore, would become Malaysia's busiest land entry point once operational, handling up to 10,000 passengers per hour in each direction.
With such volume, he said, strict emphasis must be placed on security, immigration control, threat detection and operational efficiency from day one.
"This is why safety, immigration control, threat detection and operational efficiency are top priorities for the ministry," he said.
Saifuddin said the ministry's core responsibility is not only to ensure smooth mobility of people and goods, but also to maintain strong enforcement, efficient service delivery, revenue collection and national security.
He added that the RTS Link represents a shift towards a fully integrated, technology-driven border management system, where security and efficiency operate in parallel rather than in conflict.
Among the security systems being deployed are 18 baggage scanners, 22 body scanners, and 10 walkthrough millimetre-wave scanners currently being installed and tested to enhance threat detection capabilities at the border.
Saifuddin said the system is designed to prevent security lapses rather than react to them.
"We do not want to wait until something happens before we act. Prevention is key," he said.
He added that the integrated system also supports four core functions of border management, namely seamless movement of people and goods, efficient service delivery, revenue collection, and national security, all of which will be strengthened through real-time data analytics.
Meanwhile, in Singapore, Second Minister for Home Affairs Edwin Tong told Parliament that both countries have agreed on concurrent criminal jurisdiction for the cross-border rail.
Under the framework, the destination country will hold primary jurisdiction for offences committed on board trains in transit. For incidents occurring on the 17-metre boundary stretch between Malaysia's Pier 47 and Singapore's Pier 48, authority will be determined by the train's exact position.
For emergencies, both sides have established protocols to decide which authority takes charge based on where the train is located, with mutual assistance arrangements in place and joint standard operating procedures already finalised ahead of operations.
Joint exercises will also be conducted later this year as both countries prepare for the RTS Link's opening, which is expected to become a major test of cross-border coordination, enforcement and passenger management at scale.
Malaysian border laws will apply within the designated Customs, Immigration and Quarantine (CIQ) zone at Singapore's Woodlands North station under the RTS Link, where Malaysian officers will carry out immigration and customs checks for passengers travelling to Malaysia.
However, Singapore law will remain in force in the same area, with Malaysian officers' powers limited strictly to official duties and supported by agreed legal protections and immunities under the co-location framework.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heavy rain triggers flash floods in parts of Klang Valley [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Several parts of the Klang Valley were hit by flash floods after hours of heavy rain since noon, causing traffic congestion and disruptions for commuters across Kuala Lumpur and Selangor.
On Threads, user @tharsh.h shared that roads in Ampang were inundated following the downpour.
"Is it raining across the whole of Malaysia? Ampang is flooded," he wrote.
View on Threads
Another user,
@imalmast
, shared a similar situation in Meru, Klang.
"Today at 1pm in Meru. Luckily, I managed to switch my Myvi into 'jetski' mode just in time," the user posted.
Another user, @shazwawan, shared the situation in Setia Alam.
View on Threads
"At 1.35pm, water levels are rising in front of NSK in Setia Alam. Don't make a U-turn or you might get stuck in the flood."
User
@aimizalikha_
lamented their luck in posts showing flooding in parts of Klang, including Jalan Hamzah,  nearby industrial areas.
Earlier today, the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) issued a thunderstorm warning for seven states and the Federal Territories of Putrajaya and Labuan until 1pm.
Heavy rain and strong winds were expected to affect Perak, Selangor, Negri Sembilan, Melaka, Johor, Sarawak and Sabah.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Future of RM1b Jelutong landfill project in limbo after 5th EIA rejection
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang government has yet to decide on the future of the RM1 billion Jelutong landfill rehabilitation and reclamation project following the fifth rejection of its Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA).
State Local Government Committee chairman H'ng Mooi Lye said the matter would be brought to the state executive council (Exco) for discussion after the developer submits an extension of time (EOT) request.
He said the latest EIA application was rejected by the Department of Environment (DoE) on March 2 due to unresolved technical issues.
"At this point, there is still no decision whether the project will be extended or stopped. We need to look at the full picture and consider all factors before any decision is made," he told reporters on the sidelines of the state legislative assembly sitting today.
H'ng said PDC, as the project implementing agency, is expected to table a paper on the EOT application at the upcoming Exco meeting on May 20.
"We are still waiting for a full paper from PDC on the request by PLB Engineering Bhd. The Exco will then deliberate on whether the conditions imposed by the DoE can be met," he added.
He also confirmed that the developer had proposed additional components to the project, including the possibility of a public recreational park, which would be reviewed by the state.
"We will also look at whether the updated proposals are viable and in line with regulatory requirements," he said.
The issue has sparked renewed public concern following a statement by the Protect Karpal Singh Drive Action Committee, which urged the state government to uphold an earlier commitment by Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow not to grant further extensions if EIA approval was not obtained by Feb 26.
The group claimed that, based on the DoE's EKAS database, the project has failed to obtain EIA approval five consecutive times over a period exceeding 680 days.
It said the most recent submission, dated March 18, was also recorded as "Tidak Diluluskan" (Not Approved), bringing the total rejections to five.
The committee described the repeated failures as a clear regulatory signal that the project required urgent reconsideration, particularly its coastal reclamation component.
The committee also questioned how a fifth EIA submission was accepted after the expiry of the stated deadline, and called for greater transparency on approvals and extensions granted to the developer.
The group maintained that while it supports the rehabilitation of the Jelutong landfill site, it opposes the coastal reclamation element, which it said lacked public consent and had received overwhelming objections during consultation.
The state government is expected to make further clarifications after the upcoming Exco meeting.
It was previously reported that the proposed RM1 billion landfill rehabilitation and reclamation project off Karpal Singh Drive had hit a
major roadblock after the DoE did not approve
its EIA report.
The Jelutong Waste Disposal Site (TPSJ), located beside Jalan Perusahaan Jelutong on state-owned land, ceased operations last November to allow rehabilitation work at the existing facility.
The site was used for construction and demolition waste, as well as excavated soil.
Penang had announced in 2020 its plan to rehabilitate and redevelop the 34ha landfill, which had been in operation for more than 40 years.
The RM1 billion project was to be undertaken by PLB Engineering Bhd (PLBEB).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar proposes Asean regional food security plan amid global crises
Article:
CEBU: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has proposed that Asean establish a regional food security plan as part of preparations to face global crises that could disrupt food supplies and price stability across the region.
Anwar said relevant Asean ministers should immediately explore mechanisms for regional food stockpiling during times of crisis to ensure faster coordination and emergency response.
He added that the ongoing energy crisis is already having a direct impact on food security, as rising fuel prices increase the cost of fertiliser, transportation and food production, creating supply pressures across the bloc.
If global disruptions continue, he warned that Asean could face declining food production and prolonged food inflation into next year.
"Food security must be treated as a regional strategic priority.
"Asean must also strengthen the Asean Plus Three Emergency Rice Reserve (APTERR) mechanism and consider expanding cooperation beyond rice stockpiles to include fertiliser security," he said during his speech at the 48th Asean Summit plenary session here today.
Meanwhile, the prime minister said the escalating conflict in the Middle East also serves as a reminder of Asean's shared responsibility to protect its citizens in affected areas.
"Millions of our citizens live and work across the Middle East, and their safety must remain a shared concern.
"From migrant workers and seafarers to peacekeepers and students, the risks arising from regional instability require stronger coordination among us."
In this regard, Anwar said that Asean must strengthen information-sharing, consular cooperation and emergency response mechanisms to safeguard the welfare of people in the region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dzulkefly: No Malaysians on cruise ship linked to Hantavirus outbreak
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Health Ministry (MoH) has confirmed that no Malaysians were on board an international cruise ship recently linked to a reported Hantavirus outbreak.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said communication with Singaporean health authorities revealed that two Singaporean crew members from the vessel tested negative for the virus.
"I want to provide an assurance to the community that no Malaysians were on that cruise ship," he said in a statement on his official Facebook page today.
He said that while the situation is contained, the ministry remains at its highest level of preparedness to monitor the development of the outbreak at both global and regional levels.
He explained that the virus presents as Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in the Americas, which primarily attacks the lungs.
In Europe and Asia, the infection is more likely to manifest as Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS), targeting the kidneys and blood vessels.
He warned that the virus carries a high fatality risk, noting that mortality rates for HPS can reach between 30 and 40 per cent.
"This infection should not be taken lightly as its complications have the potential to be fatal," he cautioned.
Transmission typically occurs through the inhalation of air or dust contaminated with the urine, saliva, or droppings of infected rodents.
While humans can contract the virus through direct contact with rodents or their nests, he emphasised that human-to-human transmission is extremely rare.
Early symptoms of the infection include fever, chills, severe muscle aches, headaches, and general lethargy.
More severe late-stage symptoms include shortness of breath, persistent coughing, low blood pressure, and acute kidney complications.
He said that HPS develops rapidly, requiring immediate medical intervention to prevent death.
Diagnosis is currently confirmed via blood tests, as there is no specific antiviral medication currently available to treat the virus.
"As there is no specific antiviral medicine at this time, prevention and self-control measures are our most powerful shields," he added.
Medical protocols currently rely on supportive care, such as the administration of oxygen and fluids, which may require intensive care unit monitoring.
He urged the public to maintain strict hygiene at homes and workplaces to eliminate rodent infestations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah reefs at high risk of coral bleaching, says Reef Check Malaysia
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Sabah's coral reefs are facing a high risk of bleaching according to a recent satellite imaging alert.
Reef Check Malaysia senior programme manager Adzmin Fatta said the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Satellite Bleaching Alert for Sabah had escalated from Watch to Warning level.
"In Sabah, Reef Check Malaysia is working closely with Sabah Parks, the Sabah Fisheries Department and the Sabah Biodiversity Centre to monitor the situation closely.
"We have also prepared our local Community Marine Conservation Groups to be deployed to conduct permanent bleaching monitoring protocols once activated," he said in a Facebook post.
According to the Reef Check Malaysia website, coral bleaching is an ocean distress signal caused by environmental changes.
Bleaching occurs when corals expel the symbiotic algae living in their tissues, causing them to turn white.
The loss of the algae, which provide essential nutrients and vibrant colours, threatens the survival of coral reefs, which are crucial to marine biodiversity.
Adzmin said this was the time to intensify awareness efforts, monitoring and reporting.
"We have also developed a standardised bleaching monitoring protocol – get in touch if you need guidance.
"Structured monitoring becomes critical if bleaching exceeds 20 per cent during this alert phase," he said.
Yesterday, during a visit to Sabah's east coast, Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor said while marine parks such as Sipadan Island were being managed sustainably, other islands required urgent attention.
He said Mabul and Kapalai islands were facing increasing pressure from congestion and rapid development.
"If not managed properly, we risk damaging the natural assets that are the state's economic goldmine.
"Sustainability must be seen as an investment, not a burden," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia is expensive? Local tourism group say perception matters more than numbers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — Claims on Indonesian social media suggesting Malaysia has become too expensive for tourists have raised concern among tourism industry players, though arrivals have not been significantly affected.
Malaysian Association of Tour and Travel Agents president Datuk Mohd Khalid Harun said the narrative could still influence how potential visitors perceive the country,
Harian Metro
reported today.
“Today’s tourists are more inclined to look for authentic experiences that can be enjoyed on a moderate budget,” Khalid was quoted as saying.
He said Malaysia remains a strong destination for Indonesian travellers due to cultural proximity, language familiarity, halal food options, frequent flights and family ties.
He added that negative perceptions circulating online should be monitored closely as they could affect tourism behaviour if left unchecked.
“In my view, social media posts from Indonesia about rising food prices in Malaysia do need attention, but they have not yet reached a level that would cause a sudden major impact on Malaysia’s tourism sector,” he said.
Mohd Khalid said similar cost pressures are being felt across the region due to global inflation and weaker currencies.
“What is happening now is not unique to Malaysia, as most Asean countries are also facing higher operating costs, food prices and rental rates due to global inflation and weaker regional currencies.”
He said tourists often base impressions on prices at viral eateries or tourist hotspots, which can distort the overall perception of affordability.
“When tourists see prices at popular tourist spots or viral restaurants, they may assume that the whole of Malaysia is expensive.”
He added that while arrivals may remain stable, spending patterns and trip duration could still shift.
“The most significant impact may not be a sharp drop in tourist arrivals, but rather changes in spending patterns and length of stay.”
Mohd Khalid said Malaysia must respond with stronger promotion of budget-friendly travel options and “value tourism” offerings to maintain competitiveness in the region.

Title: Queen praises Uzbekistan’s beauty, Islamic heritage and hospitality
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia has expressed admiration for Uzbekistan’s architectural beauty, Islamic heritage and the warm hospitality of its people during her recent visit to the Central Asian nation.
In a post titled “Rahmat, Uzbekistan!” dated May 7 and shared on the Facebook page of Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar today, Raja Zarith Sofiah described the visit as a meaningful experience filled with fond memories.
“For a long time, many people, including myself, only thought of travelling to Western countries. We did not know enough about the countries of Central Asia.
“I returned to Malaysia with beautiful memories of Uzbekistan. Everyone we met showed us extraordinary warmth and hospitality. Compared with some places I have visited, the cities were exceptionally clean,” Her Majesty said.
The Queen also praised the cleanliness and beauty of cities such as Tashkent and Samarkand, noting their flowers and public parks.
Raja Zarith Sofiah said she was captivated by the local craftsmanship displayed at souvenir shops in Tashkent, including intricate wood carvings, detailed paintings and colourful embroidered textiles reflecting the artistic heritage of the Uzbek people.
“There were also scarves, jackets and long robes embroidered in vibrant colours. Visiting the Centre for Islamic Civilisation was an experience I will always remember and cherish.
“I wish I had more time to see all the exhibits. The centre truly reflects the vast knowledge of scholars from the Islamic Golden Age,” Her Majesty said.
The Queen also expressed admiration for the architecture of the three main buildings at Registan Square in Samarkand, saying both the exterior and interior of the mosques and madrasahs left a lasting impression on her.
“I enjoy sketching occasionally and felt humbled by the artistry and craftsmanship of those who built the structures and surrounding areas.
“The Imam Bukhari Complex once again showcased the artistic talents, heritage and traditions of the Uzbek people. I especially admired the carved wooden pillars. I will always treasure the moment of being at the maqam of one of Islam’s greatest scholars,” according to the post.
Her Majesty said the interactive museum at the complex provided deeper insight into the life and contributions of prominent Islamic scholar Imam al-Bukhari, whose compilation of hadith continues to guide Muslims worldwide.
Raja Zarith Sofiah also expressed hope of returning to Uzbekistan with her family in the future and conveyed appreciation to the people of the country for their hospitality and sharing of knowledge throughout the visit. — Bernama

Title: MBPJ activates 24-hour flood hotline as heavy rain triggers flash floods in Petaling Jaya
Article:
PETALING JAYA, May 8 — The Petaling Jaya City Council (MBPJ) activated its disaster operations room and emergency hotline following continuous heavy rain and storm conditions today.
In a Facebook statement, MBPJ urged the public to report incidents such as fallen trees, flash floods and other weather-related emergencies through its 24-hour hotline at 03-7957 4113/03-7954 2020.
The move comes after the Malaysian Meteorological Department earlier issued a thunderstorm warning covering the Klang Valley, with heavy rain, strong winds and storm conditions forecast across several states until evening.
The adverse weather has already led to flash floods, fallen trees and traffic congestion in parts of Petaling Jaya and other parts of Selangor and Kuala Lumpur.
MBPJ said its rapid response teams have been deployed to affected locations while clean-up works are being carried out by waste management contractors.
Separately, the council said it is working with the Drainage and Irrigation Department on long-term flood mitigation measures along Sungai Penchala and Sungai Klang.
The planned upgrades include flood barriers, overflow control systems and improved drainage management to reduce the impact of recurring flash floods.

Title: Baru Bian moves to strike out PKR’s RM10m lawsuit over party exit
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — Ba’kelalan Assemblyman Baru Bian has filed an application to strike out a lawsuit brought against him by Parti Keadilan Rakyat (PKR), which is seeking RM10 million in damages over an alleged breach of bond conditions after he left the party following the 14th General Election (GE14) in 2018.
The notice was filed through Messrs YH Teh & Quek on April 6 on the grounds that the High Court of Malaya in Peninsular Malaysia does not have jurisdiction to hear the case because the cause of action arose in Sarawak.
The court has fixed May 20 for case management before High Court judge Roslan Mat Nor.
In his application, Baru Bian, who is also a former Member of Parliament (MP) for Selangau, said he signed the bond in Kuching on April 25, 2018 and that his involvement with PKR was solely centred in Sarawak.
“The plaintiff’s actions against me, even if proven true, do not arise in Peninsular Malaysia. The claim arises in Sarawak,” he stated in the application.
Baru Bian is among 16 former MPs and assemblymen being sued by PKR secretary-general Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh on behalf of the party for allegedly breaching the terms of a RM10 million bond signed with PKR.
Others named as defendants are Datuk Kamarudin Jaffar, Datuk Mansor Othman, Datuk Mohd Rashid Hasnon, Datuk Seri Saifuddin Abdullah and Datuk Seri R. Santhara Kumar.
Also named are Abdul Yunus Jamhari, Datuk Azman Nasrudin, Dr Afif Bahardin, Chong Fat Full, Daroyah Alwi, Haniza Mohamed Talha, Muhammad Hilman Idham, Muhammad Jailani Khamis, Robert Ling Kui Ee and Zulkifli Ibrahim.
In separate suits filed on February 19 and 27, PKR claimed that under the operative clause of the bond, each defendant had agreed to pay RM10 million to the party within seven days of triggering events such as resigning from the party, joining another political party or becoming an independent elected representative.
The plaintiff said it had nominated each defendant as a candidate in GE14, where they successfully won parliamentary and state seats, contributing to the formation of the federal government and several state governments.
However, on February 24, 2020, each defendant ceased to be a party member after being elected under the party banner. They also expressed support for political coalitions other than PKR,” the party stated in its statement of claim.
PKR further stated that on March 11, 2020, the party decided to terminate the membership of each defendant with immediate effect.
Certificates dated March 16, 2020, were subsequently issued confirming the termination of their memberships, along with demands for payment of the RM10 million bond, which the defendants allegedly failed to settle. — Bernama

Title: Bank Negara’s international reserves at RM508.4b as of April 30
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 8 — Bank Negara Malaysia’s (BNM) international reserves stood at US$129.7 billion (about RM508.4 billion) as at April 30, 2026.
In a statement today, the central bank said the reserves position is sufficient to finance 4.7 months of imports of goods and services, and is 0.9 times the total short-term external debt.
BNM said the gross international reserves comprise foreign currency reserves (US$113.8 billion), International Monetary Fund (IMF) reserves position (US$1.3 billion), special drawing rights (US$5.9 billion), gold (US$6.4 billion) and other reserve assets (US$2.3 billion).
Meanwhile, the central bank said its total assets amounted to RM615.55 billion, comprising mainly gold, foreign financial assets and other reserves, including special drawing rights (RM523.69 billion), Malaysian government papers (RM13.10 billion), loans and advances (RM28.92 billion), deposits with financial institutions (RM340.61 million), property and equipment (RM4.55 billion) and other assets (RM44.95 billion).
On the liabilities side, currency in circulation stood at RM184.20 billion, followed by deposits by financial institutions (RM124.98 billion), the federal government (RM4.10 billion) and others (RM70.51 billion).
The central bank’s capital stood at RM100 million, with reserves amounting to RM194.26 billion, alongside BNM papers (RM7.58 billion), allocation of special drawing rights (RM26.38 billion) and other liabilities (RM3.43 billion). — Bernama

Title: Will political funding Bill be ready before GE16? Law minister Azalina says ‘I honestly do not know’
Article:
